explanation: The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' is interpreted as 'whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold.' In LTL, this is typically captured by saying that globally, if prop_0 is true at any time, then prop_1 must be true at that same time.

The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' in natural language is interpreted as 'whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time'. This is a logical implication that must hold globally. In LTL, this is expressed using the implication operator inside a global (G) modality.

The natural language states 'If prop_0, prop_1', which can be interpreted as 'Whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time'. In LTL, this is expressed as globally (G) if prop_0 is true then prop_1 is also true: G (prop_0 -> prop_1).
The sentence 'If prop_0, prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is expressed as globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must hold as well.
The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' expresses that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is captured globally as 'globally, prop_0 implies prop_1'.
The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' is interpreted as: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time point. In LTL, this is captured globally as G (prop_0 -> prop_1).

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' suggests a conditional: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is usually captured as 'globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds'—that is, G (prop_0 -> prop_1).

The natural language statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' indicates a conditional relationship: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that point. In LTL, this is represented by the globally (G) operator: G(prop_0 -> prop_1). This ensures that at all times, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.
The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' translates to 'if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds'. In LTL, this implication can be represented globally: 'whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true'.
The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is captured by the globally operator: G (prop_0 -> prop_1).
